infobox.value: [England](/wiki/England "England") (except Cornwall and the extreme north-west), southern and eastern [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), and some localities in the eastern fringes of modern [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales")

[5th century](/wiki/5th_century "5th century") –[12th century](/wiki/12th_century "12th century"); mostly developed into [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") and [Early Scots](/wiki/Early_Scots "Early Scots") by the 12th century

[Indo-European](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages")  * [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages")   + [West Germanic](/wiki/West_Germanic "West Germanic")     - [North Sea Germanic](/wiki/North_Sea_Germanic "North Sea Germanic")       * [Anglo-Frisian](/wiki/Anglo-Frisian "Anglo-Frisian")         + [Anglic](/wiki/Anglic_languages "Anglic languages")           - **Old English**

* [Kentish](/wiki/Kentish_Old_English "Kentish Old English") * Anglian   • [Mercian](/wiki/Mercian_dialect "Mercian dialect")   • [Northumbrian](/wiki/Northumbrian_Old_English "Northumbrian Old English") * [West Saxon](/wiki/West_Saxon_dialect "West Saxon dialect")
[Runic](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_runes "Anglo-Saxon runes"), later [Latin](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script") ([Old English Latin alphabet](/wiki/Old_English_Latin_alphabet "Old English Latin alphabet"))

**Old English** (*Englisc* or *Ænglisc*, pronounced [[ˈeŋɡliʃ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Old_English "Help:IPA/Old English") or [[ˈæŋɡliʃ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Old_English "Help:IPA/Old English")), or **Anglo-Saxon**,[[a]](#cite_note-Name-2) is the earliest recorded form of the [English language](/wiki/English_language "English language"), spoken in [England](/wiki/England "England") and southern and eastern [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") in the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"). It developed from the languages brought to [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") by [Anglo-Saxon settlers](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_settlement_of_Britain "Anglo-Saxon settlement of Britain") in the mid-5th century, and the first [Old English literature](/wiki/Old_English_literature "Old English literature") dates from the mid-7th century. After the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest") of 1066, English was replaced for several centuries by [Anglo-Norman](/wiki/Anglo-Norman_language "Anglo-Norman language") (a [type of French](/wiki/Langues_d%27o%C3%AFl "Langues d'oïl")) as the language of the upper classes. This is regarded as marking the end of the Old English era, since during the subsequent period the English language was heavily influenced by Anglo-Norman, developing into what is now known as [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") in England and [Early Scots](/wiki/Early_Scots "Early Scots") in Scotland.

Old English developed from a set of [Anglo-Frisian](/wiki/Anglo-Frisian "Anglo-Frisian") or [Ingvaeonic](/wiki/Ingvaeonic "Ingvaeonic") dialects originally spoken by [Germanic tribes](/wiki/Germanic_tribes "Germanic tribes") traditionally known as the [Angles](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)"), [Saxons](/wiki/Saxons "Saxons") and [Jutes](/wiki/Jutes "Jutes"). As the Germanic settlers became dominant in England, their language [replaced](/wiki/Celtic_language_decline_in_England "Celtic language decline in England") the languages of [Roman Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain"): [Common Brittonic](/wiki/Common_Brittonic "Common Brittonic"), a [Celtic language](/wiki/Celtic_language "Celtic language"); and [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), brought to Britain by the [Roman conquest](/wiki/Roman_conquest_of_Britain "Roman conquest of Britain"). Old English had four main dialects, associated with particular [Anglo-Saxon kingdoms](/wiki/Heptarchy "Heptarchy"): [Kentish](/wiki/Kentish_Old_English "Kentish Old English"), [Mercian](/wiki/Mercian_dialect "Mercian dialect"), [Northumbrian](/wiki/Northumbrian_Old_English "Northumbrian Old English"), and [West Saxon](/wiki/West_Saxon_dialect "West Saxon dialect"). It was West Saxon that formed the basis for the literary standard of the later Old English period,[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaugh196060–83,_110–130-3) although the dominant forms of Middle and [Modern English](/wiki/Modern_English "Modern English") would develop mainly from Mercian,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and Scots from Northumbrian. The speech of eastern and northern parts of England was subject to strong [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse") influence due to [Scandinavian rule](/wiki/Danelaw "Danelaw") and settlement beginning in the 9th century.

Old English is one of the [West Germanic languages](/wiki/West_Germanic_languages "West Germanic languages"), with its closest relatives being [Old Frisian](/wiki/Old_Frisian "Old Frisian") and [Old Saxon](/wiki/Old_Saxon "Old Saxon"). Like other old Germanic languages, it is very different from Modern English and Modern Scots, and largely incomprehensible for Modern English or Modern Scots speakers without study.[[3]](#cite_note-4) Within [Old English grammar](/wiki/Old_English_grammar "Old English grammar"), the nouns, adjectives, pronouns, and verbs have many [inflectional](/wiki/Inflection "Inflection") endings and forms, and [word order](/wiki/Word_order "Word order") is much freer.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaugh196060–83,_110–130-3) The oldest Old English inscriptions were written using a [runic system](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_runes "Anglo-Saxon runes"), but from about the 8th century this was replaced by a [version of the Latin alphabet](/wiki/Old_English_Latin_alphabet "Old English Latin alphabet").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_English&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

*Englisċ*, from which the word *English* is derived, means 'pertaining to the [Angles](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)")'.[[4]](#cite_note-5) The Angles were one of the [Germanic tribes](/wiki/Germanic_tribe "Germanic tribe") who settled in many parts of Britain in the 5th century.[[5]](#cite_note-6) By the 9th century, all speakers of Old English, including those who claimed Saxon or Jutish ancestry, could be referred to as *Englisċ*.

This name probably either derives from [Proto-Germanic](/wiki/Proto-Germanic "Proto-Germanic") *[\*anguz](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Reconstruction:Proto-Germanic/anguz#Proto-Germanic "wikt:Reconstruction:Proto-Germanic/anguz")*, which referred to narrowness, constriction or anxiety, perhaps referring to shallow waters near the coast,[[6]](#cite_note-7) or else it may derive from a related word *[\*angô](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Reconstruction:Proto-Germanic/ang%C3%B4#Proto-Germanic "wikt:Reconstruction:Proto-Germanic/angô")* which could refer to curve or hook shapes including fishing hooks.[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoggDenison2006-9) Concerning the second option, it has been hypothesised that the Angles acquired their name either because they lived on a curved promontory of land shaped like a [fishhook](/wiki/Fishhook "Fishhook"), or else because they were fishermen (anglers).[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaugh1960-10)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_English&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Old English

Continental West Germanic languages ([Old Frisian](/wiki/Old_Frisian "Old Frisian"), [Old Saxon](/wiki/Old_Saxon "Old Saxon"), [Old Dutch](/wiki/Old_Dutch "Old Dutch"), [Old High German](/wiki/Old_High_German "Old High German")).

[Crimean Gothic](/wiki/Crimean_Gothic "Crimean Gothic") ([East Germanic](/wiki/East_Germanic "East Germanic"))

Old English was not static, and its usage covered a period of 700 years, from the [Anglo-Saxon settlement of Britain](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_settlement_of_Britain "Anglo-Saxon settlement of Britain") in the 5th century to the late 11th century, some time after the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest"). While indicating that the establishment of dates is an arbitrary process, Albert Baugh dates Old English from 450 to 1150, a period of full inflections, a [synthetic language](/wiki/Synthetic_language "Synthetic language").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaugh196060–83,_110–130-3) Perhaps around 85% of Old English words are no longer in use, but those that survived are the basic elements of [Modern English](/wiki/Modern_English "Modern English") vocabulary.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaugh196060–83,_110–130-3)

Old English is a [West Germanic language](/wiki/West_Germanic_language "West Germanic language"), and developed out of [North Sea Germanic](/wiki/North_Sea_Germanic "North Sea Germanic") dialects from the 5th century. It came to be spoken over most of the territory of the [Anglo-Saxon kingdoms](/wiki/Heptarchy "Heptarchy") which became the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"). This included most of present-day England, as well as part of what is now southeastern [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), which for several centuries belonged to the [kingdom of Northumbria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Northumbria "Kingdom of Northumbria"). Other parts of the island continued to use [Celtic languages](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages") ([Gaelic](/wiki/Goidelic_languages "Goidelic languages") – and perhaps some [Pictish](/wiki/Pictish "Pictish") – in most of Scotland, [Medieval Cornish](/wiki/Cornish_language#Middle_Cornish "Cornish language") all over [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall") and in adjacent parts of [Devon](/wiki/Devon "Devon"), [Cumbric](/wiki/Cumbric "Cumbric") perhaps to the 12th century in parts of [Cumbria](/wiki/Cumbria "Cumbria"), and [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language") in [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") and possibly also on the English side of the [Anglo-Welsh border](/wiki/Anglo-Welsh_border "Anglo-Welsh border")); except in the areas of Scandinavian settlements, where [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse") was spoken and [Danish law](/wiki/Danelaw "Danelaw") applied.

Old English literacy developed after the [Christianisation of Anglo-Saxon England](/wiki/Christianisation_of_Anglo-Saxon_England "Christianisation of Anglo-Saxon England") in the late 7th century. The oldest surviving work of [Old English literature](/wiki/Old_English_literature "Old English literature") is *[Cædmon's Hymn](/wiki/C%C3%A6dmon%27s_Hymn "Cædmon's Hymn")*, which was composed between 658 and 680 but not written down until the early 8th century.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaugh196060–83,_110–130-3) There is a limited corpus of [runic inscriptions](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_runes "Anglo-Saxon runes") from the 5th to 7th centuries, but the oldest coherent runic texts (notably the inscriptions on the [Franks Casket](/wiki/Franks_Casket "Franks Casket")) date to the early 8th century. The [Old English Latin alphabet](/wiki/Old_English_Latin_alphabet "Old English Latin alphabet") was introduced around the 8th century.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Statue_d%27Alfred_le_Grand_%C3%A0_Winchester.jpg/250px-Statue_d%27Alfred_le_Grand_%C3%A0_Winchester.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statue_d%27Alfred_le_Grand_%C3%A0_Winchester.jpg)

[Alfred the Great](/wiki/Alfred_the_Great "Alfred the Great") statue in [Winchester](/wiki/Winchester "Winchester"), Hampshire. The 9th-century English King proposed that primary education be taught in English, with those wishing to advance to holy orders to continue their studies in Latin.

With the unification of several of the Anglo-Saxon kingdoms (outside the [Danelaw](/wiki/Danelaw "Danelaw")) by [Alfred the Great](/wiki/Alfred_the_Great "Alfred the Great") in the later 9th century, the language of government and literature became standardised around the [West Saxon dialect](/wiki/West_Saxon_dialect "West Saxon dialect") (Early West Saxon). Alfred [advocated education in English](/wiki/Alfred_the_Great#Advocacy_of_education_in_English "Alfred the Great") alongside Latin, and had many works translated into the English language; some of them, such as [Pope Gregory I](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_I "Pope Gregory I")'s treatise *[Pastoral Care](/wiki/Pastoral_Care "Pastoral Care")*, appear to have been translated by Alfred himself. In Old English, typical of the development of literature, poetry arose before prose, but Alfred chiefly inspired the growth of prose.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaugh196060–83,_110–130-3)

A later literary standard, dating from the late 10th century, arose under the influence of Bishop [Æthelwold of Winchester](/wiki/%C3%86thelwold_of_Winchester "Æthelwold of Winchester"), and was followed by such writers as the prolific [Ælfric of Eynsham](/wiki/%C3%86lfric_of_Eynsham "Ælfric of Eynsham") ("the Grammarian"). This form of the language is known as the "[Winchester](/wiki/Winchester "Winchester") standard", or more commonly as Late West Saxon. It is considered to represent the "classical" form of Old English.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHogg199283-11) It retained its position of prestige until the time of the Norman Conquest, after which English ceased for a time to be of importance as a literary language.

The history of Old English can be subdivided into:

* Prehistoric Old English (c. 450–650); for this period, Old English is mostly a [reconstructed language](/wiki/Reconstructed_language "Reconstructed language") as no literary witnesses survive (with the exception of limited [epigraphic evidence](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_runes "Anglo-Saxon runes")). This language, or closely related group of dialects, spoken by the Angles, Saxons, and Jutes, and pre-dating documented Old English or Anglo-Saxon, has also been called Primitive Old English.[[11]](#cite_note-12)
* Early Old English (c. 650–900), the period of the oldest manuscript traditions, with authors such as [Cædmon](/wiki/C%C3%A6dmon "Cædmon"), [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede"), [Cynewulf](/wiki/Cynewulf "Cynewulf") and [Aldhelm](/wiki/Aldhelm "Aldhelm").
* Late Old English (c. 900–1150), the final stage of the language leading up to the Norman conquest of England and the subsequent transition to [Early Middle English](/wiki/Early_Middle_English "Early Middle English").[[12]](#cite_note-13)

The Old English period is followed by [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") (1150–1500), [Early Modern English](/wiki/Early_Modern_English "Early Modern English") (1500–1650) and finally [Modern English](/wiki/Modern_English "Modern English") (after 1650), and in Scotland [Early Scots](/wiki/Early_Scots "Early Scots") (before 1450), [Middle Scots](/wiki/Middle_Scots "Middle Scots") (c. 1450–1700) and [Modern Scots](/wiki/Modern_Scots "Modern Scots") (after 1700).

## Dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_English&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Dialects")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Old_English_Dialects.png/250px-Old_English_Dialects.png)](/wiki/File:Old_English_Dialects.png)

The dialects of Old English c. 800 CE

Just as [Modern English](/wiki/Modern_English "Modern English") is not monolithic, Old English varied according to place. Despite the diversity of language of the Germanic-speaking migrants who established Old English in England and southeastern Scotland, it is possible to reconstruct proto-Old English as a fairly unitary language. For the most part, the differences between the attested regional dialects of Old English developed within England and southeastern Scotland, rather than on the Mainland of Europe. Although from the tenth century Old English writing from all regions tended to conform to a [written standard](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language") based on Late West Saxon, in speech Old English continued to exhibit much local and regional variation, which remained in Middle English and to some extent [Modern English dialects](/wiki/English_dialects "English dialects").[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampbell1959§§5–22-14)

The four main dialectal forms of Old English were [Mercian](/wiki/Mercian_dialect "Mercian dialect"), [Northumbrian](/wiki/Northumbrian_Old_English "Northumbrian Old English"), [Kentish](/wiki/Kentish_Old_English "Kentish Old English"), and [West Saxon](/wiki/West_Saxon_dialect "West Saxon dialect").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampbell19594-15) Mercian and Northumbrian are together referred to as *Anglian*. In terms of geography the Northumbrian region lay north of the Humber River; the Mercian lay north of the [Thames](/wiki/Thames "Thames") and south of the Humber River; West Saxon lay south and southwest of the Thames; and the smallest, Kentish region lay southeast of the Thames, a small corner of England. The Kentish region, settled by the Jutes from Jutland, has the scantest literary remains.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaugh196060–83,_110–130-3) The term *West Saxon* actually is represented by two different dialects: Early West Saxon and Late West Saxon. Hogg has suggested that these two dialects would be more appropriately named *Alfredian Saxon* and *Æthelwoldian Saxon*, respectively, so that the naive reader would not assume that they are chronologically related.

Each of these four dialects was associated with an independent kingdom on the islands. Of these, Northumbria south of the [Tyne](/wiki/River_Tyne "River Tyne"), and most of [Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia"), were [overrun by the Vikings](/wiki/Viking_invasion_of_Britain "Viking invasion of Britain") during the 9th century. The portion of Mercia that was successfully defended, and all of [Kent](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kent "Kingdom of Kent"), were then integrated into Wessex under [Alfred the Great](/wiki/Alfred_the_Great "Alfred the Great").
From that time on, the West Saxon dialect (then in the form now known as Early West Saxon) became standardised as the language of government, and as the basis for the many works of literature and religious materials produced or translated from Latin in that period.

The later literary standard known as Late West Saxon (see [§ History](#History)), although centred in the same region of the country, appears not to have been directly descended from Alfred's Early West Saxon. For example, the former [diphthong](/wiki/Diphthong "Diphthong") /iy/ tended to become monophthongised to /i/ in EWS, but to /y/ in LWS.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHogg1992117-16)

Due to the centralisation of power and the destruction wrought by Viking invasions, there is relatively little written record of the non-West Saxon dialects after Alfred's unification. Some Mercian texts continued to be written, however, and the influence of Mercian is apparent in some of the translations produced under Alfred's programme, many of which were produced by Mercian scholars.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagennis201156–60-17) Other dialects certainly continued to be spoken, as is evidenced by the continued variation between their successors in Middle and Modern English. In fact, what would become the standard forms of Middle English and of Modern English are descended from Mercian rather than West Saxon, while [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language") developed from the Northumbrian dialect.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It was once claimed that, owing to its position at the heart of the Kingdom of Wessex, the relics of Anglo-Saxon accent, idiom and vocabulary were best preserved in the [dialect of Somerset](/wiki/West_Country_dialects#History_and_origins "West Country dialects").[[17]](#cite_note-18)

## Influence of other languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_English&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Influence of other languages")]

Further information: [Celtic influence in English](/wiki/Celtic_influence_in_English "Celtic influence in English"), [Latin influence in English](/wiki/Latin_influence_in_English "Latin influence in English"), and [Scandinavian influence in English](/wiki/Scandinavian_influence_in_English "Scandinavian influence in English")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Her_swutela%C3%B0_seo_gecwydr%C3%A6dnes_%C3%B0e.jpg/250px-Her_swutela%C3%B0_seo_gecwydr%C3%A6dnes_%C3%B0e.jpg)](/wiki/File:Her_swutela%C3%B0_seo_gecwydr%C3%A6dnes_%C3%B0e.jpg)

*Her sƿutelað seo gecƿydrædnes ðe* ('Here the Word is revealed to thee'). Old English inscription over the arch of the south [porticus](/wiki/Porticus "Porticus") in the 10th century [St Mary's parish church, Breamore](/wiki/Breamore#St_Mary's_church "Breamore"), Hampshire

The language of the Anglo-Saxon settlers appears not to have been significantly affected by the native [British Celtic languages](/wiki/British_Celtic_languages "British Celtic languages") which it [largely displaced](/wiki/Celtic_language-death_in_England "Celtic language-death in England"). The number of Celtic [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") introduced into the language is very small, although dialect and toponymic terms are more often retained in western language contact zones (Cumbria, Devon, Welsh Marches and Borders and so on) than in the east. However, various suggestions have been made concerning possible influence that Celtic may have had on developments in English [syntax](/wiki/Syntax "Syntax") in the post–Old English period, such as the regular [progressive](/wiki/Progressive_aspect "Progressive aspect") construction and [analytic word order](/wiki/Analytic_language "Analytic language"),[[18]](#cite_note-19) as well as the eventual development of the [periphrastic](/wiki/Periphrastic "Periphrastic") auxiliary verb *[do](/wiki/Do-support "Do-support")*. These ideas have generally not received widespread support from linguists, particularly as many of the theorised [Brittonicisms](/wiki/Brittonicisms_in_English "Brittonicisms in English") do not become widespread until the late Middle English and Early Modern English periods, in addition to the fact that similar forms exist in other modern Germanic languages.[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25)[[25]](#cite_note-26)

Old English contained a certain number of loanwords from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), which was the scholarly and diplomatic *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* of Western Europe. It is sometimes possible to give approximate dates for the borrowing of individual Latin words based on which patterns of sound change they have undergone. Some Latin words had already been borrowed into the Germanic languages before the ancestral [Angles](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)") and [Saxons](/wiki/Saxons "Saxons") left continental Europe for Britain. More entered the language when the Anglo-Saxons were [converted to Christianity](/wiki/Christianization_of_England "Christianization of England") and Latin-speaking priests became influential. It was also through Irish Christian missionaries that the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet") was introduced and adapted for the [writing of Old English](#Orthography), replacing the earlier runic system. Nonetheless, the largest transfer of Latin-based (mainly [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French")) words into English occurred in the [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") period.

Another source of loanwords was [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse"), which came into contact with Old English via the Scandinavian rulers and settlers in the [Danelaw](/wiki/Danelaw "Danelaw") from the late 9th century, and during the rule of [Cnut](/wiki/Cnut "Cnut") and other Danish kings in the early 11th century. Many [place names](/wiki/Place_names "Place names") in eastern and northern England are of Scandinavian origin. Norse borrowings are relatively rare in Old English literature, being mostly terms relating to government and administration. The literary standard, however, was based on the [West Saxon dialect](/wiki/West_Saxon_dialect "West Saxon dialect"), away from the main area of Scandinavian influence; the impact of Norse may have been greater in the eastern and northern dialects. Certainly in Middle English texts, which are more often based on eastern dialects, a strong Norse influence becomes apparent. Modern English contains many, often everyday, words that were borrowed from Old Norse, and the grammatical simplification that occurred after the Old English period is also often attributed to Norse influence.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaugh196060–83,_110–130-3)[[26]](#cite_note-27)[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJespersen192358–82-28)

The influence of Old Norse certainly helped move English from a [synthetic language](/wiki/Synthetic_language "Synthetic language") along the continuum to a more [analytic word order](/wiki/Analytic_language "Analytic language"), and [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse") most likely made a greater impact on the English language than any other language.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaugh196060–83,_110–130-3)[[28]](#cite_note-29) The eagerness of [Vikings](/wiki/Vikings "Vikings") in the Danelaw to communicate with their Anglo-Saxon neighbours produced a friction that led to the erosion of the complicated inflectional word endings.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJespersen192358–82-28)[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCrum198770–71-31) Simeon Potter notes:

> No less far-reaching was the influence of Scandinavian upon the inflexional endings of English in hastening that wearing away and leveling of grammatical forms which gradually spread from north to south. It was, after all, a salutary influence. The gain was greater than the loss. There was a gain in directness, in clarity, and in strength.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

The strength of the Viking influence on Old English appears from the fact that the indispensable elements of the language – [pronouns](/wiki/Pronoun "Pronoun"), [modals](/wiki/English_modal_verbs "English modal verbs"), [comparatives](/wiki/Comparison_(grammar) "Comparison (grammar)"), [pronominal adverbs](/wiki/Pronominal_adverb "Pronominal adverb") (like *hence* and *together*), [conjunctions](/wiki/Conjunction_(grammar) "Conjunction (grammar)") and [prepositions](/wiki/English_prepositions "English prepositions") – show the most marked Danish influence; the best evidence of Scandinavian influence appears in the extensive word borrowings because, as Jespersen indicates, no texts exist in either Scandinavia or Northern England from this time to give certain evidence of an influence on syntax. The effect of Old Norse on Old English was substantive, pervasive, and of a democratic character.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaugh196060–83,_110–130-3)[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJespersen192358–82-28) Old Norse and Old English resembled each other closely like cousins, and with some words in common, speakers roughly understood each other;[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJespersen192358–82-28) in time the inflections melted away and the analytic pattern emerged.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCrum198770–71-31)[[32]](#cite_note-33) It is most important to recognise that in many words the English and Scandinavian languages differed chiefly in their inflectional elements. The body of the word was so nearly the same in the two languages that only the endings would put obstacles in the way of mutual understanding. In the mixed population which existed in the Danelaw, these endings must have led to much confusion, tending gradually to become obscured and finally lost. This blending of peoples and languages resulted in "simplifying English grammar".[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaugh196060–83,_110–130-3)

* [dʒ] is an [allophone](/wiki/Allophone "Allophone") of /j/ occurring after /n/ and when [geminated](/wiki/Geminated "Geminated") (doubled).
* [ŋ] is an allophone of /n/ occurring before [k] and [ɡ].
* [v, ð, z] are voiced allophones of /f, θ, s/ respectively, occurring between [vowels](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel") or [voiced consonants](/wiki/Voiced_consonant "Voiced consonant") when the preceding sound was stressed.
* [h, ç] are allophones of /x/ occurring at the beginning of a word or after a front vowel, respectively.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The above system is largely similar to [that of Modern English](/wiki/English_phonology#Consonants "English phonology"), except that [ç, x, ɣ, l̥, n̥, r̥] (and [ʍ] for [most speakers](/wiki/Pronunciation_of_English_%E2%9F%A8wh%E2%9F%A9 "Pronunciation of English ⟨wh⟩")) have generally been lost, while the voiced affricate and fricatives (now also including /ʒ/) have become independent phonemes, as has /ŋ/.

The [open back rounded vowel](/wiki/Open_back_rounded_vowel "Open back rounded vowel") [ɒ] was an allophone of short /ɑ/ which occurred in stressed syllables before nasal consonants (/m/ and /n/). It was variously spelt either ⟨a⟩ or ⟨o⟩.

The Anglian dialects also had the [mid front rounded vowel](/wiki/Mid_front_rounded_vowel "Mid front rounded vowel") /ø(ː)/, spelled ⟨oe⟩, which had emerged from [i-umlaut](/wiki/I-umlaut "I-umlaut") of /o(ː)/. In West Saxon and Kentish, it had already merged with /e(ː)/ before the first written prose.

### Sound changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_English&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Sound changes")]

Main article: [Phonological history of Old English](/wiki/Phonological_history_of_Old_English "Phonological history of Old English")

Some of the principal [sound changes](/wiki/Sound_change "Sound change") occurring in the pre-history and history of Old English were the following:

* Fronting of [ɑ(ː)] to [æ(ː)] except when [nasalised](/wiki/Nasal_vowel "Nasal vowel") or followed by a [nasal consonant](/wiki/Nasal_consonant "Nasal consonant") ("Anglo-Frisian brightening"), partly reversed in certain positions by later "a-restoration" or retraction.
* Monophthongisation of the diphthong [ai], and modification of remaining diphthongs to the [height-harmonic](/wiki/Height-harmonic "Height-harmonic") type.
* Diphthongisation of long and short front vowels in certain positions ("breaking").
* Palatalisation of velars [k], [ɡ], [ɣ], [sk] to [tʃ], [dʒ], [j], [ʃ] in certain front-vowel environments.
* The process known as [i-mutation](/wiki/Germanic_umlaut#I-mutation_in_Old_English "Germanic umlaut") (which for example led to modern *mice* as the plural of *mouse*).
* Loss of certain weak vowels in word-final and medial positions; reduction of remaining unstressed vowels.
* Diphthongisation of certain vowels before certain consonants when preceding a back vowel ("back mutation").
* Loss of /x/ between vowels or between a voiced consonant and a vowel, with lengthening of the preceding vowel.
* Collapse of two consecutive vowels into a single vowel.
* "Palatal umlaut", which has given forms such as *six* (compare German *sechs*).

## Grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_English&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Grammar")]

Main article: [Old English grammar](/wiki/Old_English_grammar "Old English grammar")

### Morphology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_English&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Morphology")]

Nouns [decline](/wiki/Declension "Declension") for five [cases](/wiki/Grammatical_case "Grammatical case"): [nominative](/wiki/Nominative "Nominative"), [accusative](/wiki/Accusative "Accusative"), [genitive](/wiki/Genitive "Genitive"), [dative](/wiki/Dative "Dative"), [instrumental](/wiki/Instrumental_case "Instrumental case"); three [genders](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender"): masculine, feminine, neuter; and two [numbers](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number"): singular, and plural; and are strong or weak. The instrumental is vestigial and only used with the masculine and neuter singular and often replaced by the [dative](/wiki/Dative "Dative"). Only [pronouns](/wiki/Pronoun "Pronoun") and strong adjectives retain separate [instrumental](/wiki/Instrumental_case "Instrumental case") forms. There is also sparse early Northumbrian evidence of a sixth case: the [locative](/wiki/Locative "Locative"). The evidence comes from Northumbrian [Runic](/wiki/Runic "Runic") texts (e.g. ᚩᚾ ᚱᚩᛞᛁ *on rodi* 'on the Cross').[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPage1973230-38)

Adjectives agree with nouns in case, gender, and number, and can be either strong or weak. Pronouns and sometimes [participles](/wiki/Participle "Participle") agree in case, gender, and number. First-person and second-[person](/wiki/Grammatical_person "Grammatical person") pronouns occasionally distinguish [dual-number](/wiki/Dual_(grammatical_number) "Dual (grammatical number)") forms. The [definite article](/wiki/Definite_article "Definite article") *sē* and its [inflections](/wiki/Inflection "Inflection") serve as a [definite article](/wiki/Definite_article "Definite article") (*the*), a [demonstrative adjective](/wiki/Demonstrative_adjective "Demonstrative adjective") (*that*), and [demonstrative pronoun](/wiki/Demonstrative_pronoun "Demonstrative pronoun"). Other demonstratives are *þēs* ("this"), and *ġeon* ("that over there"). These words inflect for case, gender, and number. Adjectives have both strong and weak sets of endings, weak ones being used when a definite or possessive [determiner](/wiki/Determiner "Determiner") is also present.

Verbs [conjugate](/wiki/Grammatical_conjugation "Grammatical conjugation") for three [persons](/wiki/Grammatical_person "Grammatical person"): first, second, and third; two numbers: singular, plural; two [tenses](/wiki/Tenses "Tenses"): present, and past; three [moods](/wiki/Grammatical_mood "Grammatical mood"): [indicative](/wiki/Grammatical_mood#indicative "Grammatical mood"), [subjunctive](/wiki/Subjunctive "Subjunctive"), and [imperative](/wiki/Imperative_mood "Imperative mood");[[38]](#cite_note-EncyclopediaofBritishLiterature-39) and are strong (exhibiting ablaut) or weak (exhibiting a dental suffix). Verbs have two [infinitive](/wiki/Infinitive "Infinitive") forms: bare and bound; and two [participles](/wiki/Participle "Participle"): present and past. The subjunctive has past and present forms. Finite verbs agree with [subjects](/wiki/Subject_(grammar) "Subject (grammar)") in person and number. The [future tense](/wiki/Future_tense "Future tense"), [passive voice](/wiki/Passive_voice "Passive voice"), and other [aspects](/wiki/Grammatical_aspect "Grammatical aspect") are formed with compounds. [Adpositions](/wiki/Adpositions "Adpositions") are mostly before but are often after their object. If the [object](/wiki/Object_(grammar) "Object (grammar)") of an adposition is marked in the dative case, an adposition may conceivably be located anywhere in the sentence.

Remnants of the Old English case system in Modern English are in the [forms of a few pronouns](/wiki/English_personal_pronouns "English personal pronouns") (such as *I/me/mine*, *she/her*, *[who/whom/whose](/wiki/Who_(pronoun) "Who (pronoun)")*) and in the [possessive](/wiki/English_possessive "English possessive") ending *-'s*, which derives from the masculine and neuter genitive ending *-es*. The modern [English plural](/wiki/English_plural "English plural") ending *-(e)s* derives from the Old English *-as*, but the latter applied only to "strong" masculine nouns in the nominative and accusative cases; different plural endings were used in other instances. Old English nouns had [grammatical gender](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender"), while modern English has only natural gender. Pronoun usage could reflect either natural or grammatical gender when those conflicted, as in the case of the grammatically neuter (but naturally feminine) noun *ƿīf* (/wiːf/), which meant "woman" (from *ƿīfmann*, lit. 'woman person' or 'female person') and became Modern English *wife*.

In Old English's verbal compound constructions are the beginnings of the [compound tenses of Modern English](/wiki/Uses_of_English_verb_forms "Uses of English verb forms").[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMitchellRobinson2001109–112-40) Old English verbs include [strong verbs](/wiki/Germanic_strong_verb "Germanic strong verb"), which form the past tense by altering the root vowel, and [weak verbs](/wiki/Germanic_weak_verb "Germanic weak verb"), which use a suffix such as *-de*.[[38]](#cite_note-EncyclopediaofBritishLiterature-39) As in Modern English, and peculiar to the Germanic languages, the verbs formed two great classes: weak (regular), and strong (irregular). Like today, Old English had fewer strong verbs, and many of these have over time decayed into weak forms. Then, as now, dental suffixes indicated the past tense of the weak verbs, as in *work* and *worked*.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaugh196060–83,_110–130-3)

### Syntax

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_English&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Syntax")]

Old English [syntax](/wiki/Syntax "Syntax") is similar to [that of modern English](/wiki/English_grammar "English grammar"). Some differences are consequences of the greater level of nominal and verbal inflection, allowing freer [word order](/wiki/Word_order "Word order").

* Default word order is [verb-second](/wiki/Verb-second "Verb-second") in [main clauses](/wiki/Independent_clause "Independent clause"), and verb-final in [subordinate clauses](/wiki/Subordinate_clause "Subordinate clause")[[40]](#cite_note-41)
* No [*do*-support](/wiki/Do-support "Do-support") in questions and negatives. Questions were usually formed by [inverting](/wiki/Inversion_(linguistics) "Inversion (linguistics)") [subject](/wiki/Subject_(grammar) "Subject (grammar)") and [finite verb](/wiki/Finite_verb "Finite verb"), and negatives by placing *ne* before the finite verb, regardless of which verb.
* Multiple negatives can stack up in a sentence intensifying each other ([negative concord](/wiki/Negative_concord "Negative concord")).
* Sentences with subordinate clauses of the type "when X, Y" (e.g. "When I got home, I ate dinner") do not use a *wh-*type conjunction, but rather a *th-*type [correlative conjunction](/wiki/Correlative_conjunction "Correlative conjunction") such as *þā*, otherwise meaning "then" (e.g. *þā X, þā Y* in place of "when X, Y"). The *wh-*words (or "hw-words" in Old English's case) are used only as [interrogatives](/wiki/Interrogative "Interrogative") and as [indefinite pronouns](/wiki/Indefinite_pronouns "Indefinite pronouns").
* Similarly, *wh-*forms were not used as [relative pronouns](/wiki/Relative_pronoun "Relative pronoun"); instead, the indeclinable word *þe* is used, often preceded by (or replaced by) the appropriate form of the article/demonstrative *se*.

## Orthography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_English&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Orthography")]

Main articles: [Anglo-Saxon runes](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_runes "Anglo-Saxon runes") and [Old English Latin alphabet](/wiki/Old_English_Latin_alphabet "Old English Latin alphabet")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Anglosaxonrunes.svg/330px-Anglosaxonrunes.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Anglosaxonrunes.svg)

The [runic alphabet](/wiki/Runic_alphabet "Runic alphabet") used to write Old English before the introduction of the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet")

Old English was first written in [runes](/wiki/Runes "Runes"), using the [futhorc](/wiki/Futhorc "Futhorc") – a rune set derived from the Germanic 24-character [elder futhark](/wiki/Elder_futhark "Elder futhark"), extended by five more runes used to represent Anglo-Saxon vowel sounds and sometimes by several more additional characters. From around the 8th century, the runic system came to be supplanted by a (minuscule) [half-uncial](/wiki/Half-uncial "Half-uncial") script of the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet") introduced by [Irish Christian](/wiki/Hiberno-Scottish_mission "Hiberno-Scottish mission") missionaries.[[41]](#cite_note-42) This was replaced by [Insular script](/wiki/Insular_script "Insular script"), a cursive and pointed version of the half-uncial script. This was used until the end of the 12th century when continental [Carolingian minuscule](/wiki/Carolingian_minuscule "Carolingian minuscule") (also known as *Caroline*) replaced the insular.

The Latin alphabet of the time still lacked the letters ⟨j⟩ and ⟨w⟩, and there was no ⟨v⟩ as distinct from ⟨u⟩; moreover native Old English spellings did not use ⟨k⟩, ⟨q⟩ or ⟨z⟩. The remaining 20 Latin letters were supplemented by 4 more: ⟨[æ](/wiki/%C3%86 "Æ")⟩ (*æsc*, modern *ash*) and ⟨ð⟩ (*ðæt*, now called [eth](/wiki/Eth "Eth") or edh), which were modified Latin letters, and [thorn](/wiki/Thorn_(letter) "Thorn (letter)") ⟨þ⟩ and [wynn](/wiki/Wynn "Wynn") ⟨ƿ⟩, which are borrowings from the futhorc. A few letter pairs were used as [digraphs](/wiki/Digraph_(orthography) "Digraph (orthography)"), representing a single sound. Also used was the [Tironian note](/wiki/Tironian_note "Tironian note") ⟨⁊⟩ (a character similar to the digit ⟨7⟩) for the [conjunction](/wiki/Grammatical_conjunction "Grammatical conjunction") *and*. A common [scribal abbreviation](/wiki/Scribal_abbreviation "Scribal abbreviation") was a [thorn with a stroke](/wiki/Thorn_with_stroke "Thorn with stroke") ⟨ꝥ⟩, which was used for the pronoun *þæt* (*that*). [Macrons](/wiki/Macron_(diacritic) "Macron (diacritic)") over vowels were originally used not to mark long vowels (as in modern editions), but to indicate stress, or as abbreviations for a following ⟨m⟩ or ⟨n⟩.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMillwardHayes201196-43)[[43]](#cite_note-44)

Modern editions of Old English manuscripts generally introduce some additional conventions. The modern forms of Latin letters are used, including ⟨g⟩ instead of [insular *G*](/wiki/Insular_G "Insular G"), ⟨s⟩ instead of [insular *S*](/wiki/Insular_S "Insular S") and [long *S*](/wiki/Long_S "Long S"), and others which may differ considerably from the insular script, notably ⟨e⟩, ⟨f⟩ and ⟨r⟩. Macrons are used to indicate long vowels, where usually no distinction was made between long and short vowels in the originals. (In some older editions an [acute accent](/wiki/Acute_accent "Acute accent") mark was used for consistency with Old Norse conventions.) Additionally, modern editions often distinguish between [velar](/wiki/Velar_consonant "Velar consonant") and [palatal](/wiki/Palatal_consonant "Palatal consonant") ⟨c⟩ and ⟨g⟩ by placing dots above the palatals: ⟨ċ⟩, ⟨ġ⟩. The letter wynn ⟨ƿ⟩ is usually replaced with ⟨w⟩, but ⟨æ⟩, ⟨ð⟩ and ⟨þ⟩ are normally retained – except when ⟨ð⟩ is replaced by ⟨þ⟩.

In contrast with modern [English orthography](/wiki/English_orthography "English orthography"), Old English spelling was reasonably [regular](/wiki/Regular_spelling "Regular spelling"), with a mostly predictable correspondence between letters and [phonemes](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme"). There were not usually any [silent letters](/wiki/Silent_letter "Silent letter") – in the word *cniht*, for example, both the ⟨c⟩ and ⟨h⟩ were pronounced (/knixt ~ kniçt/) unlike the ⟨k⟩ and ⟨gh⟩ in the modern *knight* (/naɪt/). One exception was the occasional insertion of a silent ⟨e⟩ between a palatal consonant and an immediately following back vowel. While most of the sound changes that produced palatal consonants had occurred only before front vowels (and sometimes before /j/), later sound changes often deleted the responsible front vowel or /j/, resulting in a palatal consonant occurring in a "forbidden" position, i.e. before a back vowel. Additionally, certain cases of /j/ were inherited directly from Proto-Germanic rather than occurring due to palatalisation of /ɣ/, and so could freely occur before back vowels. To avoid ambiguity, scribes occasionally inserted the silent ⟨e⟩ to mark the "forbidden" palatal – for example, the verb *smēaġan* (/ˈsmæ͜ɑː.jɑn/), which retains an inherited /j/, was sometimes written as ⟨smeagean⟩.

## Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_English&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Literature")]

Main article: [Old English literature](/wiki/Old_English_literature "Old English literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Beowulf_Cotton_MS_Vitellius_A_XV_f._132r.jpg/250px-Beowulf_Cotton_MS_Vitellius_A_XV_f._132r.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beowulf_Cotton_MS_Vitellius_A_XV_f._132r.jpg)

The first page of the *Beowulf* manuscript with its opening  
*Hƿæt ƿē Gārde/na ingēar dagum þēod cyninga / þrym ge frunon...*  
"Listen! We of the Spear-Danes from days of yore have heard of the glory of the nation-kings..."

The corpus of Old English literature is small but still significant, with some 400 surviving manuscripts.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKer1990-55) The pagan and Christian streams mingle in Old English, one of the richest and most significant bodies of literature preserved among the early Germanic peoples.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaugh196060–83,_110–130-3) In his supplementary article to the 1935 posthumous edition of Bright's *Anglo-Saxon Reader*, James Hulbert writes:

> In such historical conditions, an incalculable amount of the writings of the Anglo-Saxon period perished. What they contained, how important they were for an understanding of literature before the Conquest, we have no means of knowing: the scant catalogues of monastic libraries do not help us, and there are no references in extant works to other compositions....How incomplete our materials are can be illustrated by the well-known fact that, with few and relatively unimportant exceptions, all extant Anglo-Saxon poetry is preserved in four manuscripts.

Some of the most important surviving works of Old English literature are *[Beowulf](/wiki/Beowulf "Beowulf")*, an [epic poem](/wiki/Epic_poem "Epic poem"); the *[Anglo-Saxon Chronicle](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_Chronicle "Anglo-Saxon Chronicle")*, a record of early English history; the [Franks Casket](/wiki/Franks_Casket "Franks Casket"), an inscribed early whalebone artefact; and [Cædmon's Hymn](/wiki/C%C3%A6dmon%27s_Hymn "Cædmon's Hymn"), a Christian religious poem. There are also a number of extant prose works, such as sermons and saints' lives, biblical translations, and translated Latin works of the early Church Fathers, legal documents, such as laws and wills, and practical works on grammar, medicine, and geography. Still, poetry is considered the heart of Old English literature. Nearly all Anglo-Saxon authors are anonymous, with a few exceptions, such as [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede") and [Cædmon](/wiki/C%C3%A6dmon "Cædmon"). Cædmon, the earliest English poet known by name, served as a lay brother in the monastery at Whitby.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaugh196060–83,_110–130-3)

The first example is taken from the opening lines of *Beowulf*, a work with around 3,000 lines.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaugh196060–83,_110–130-3) This passage describes how [Hrothgar](/wiki/Hrothgar "Hrothgar")'s legendary ancestor [Scyld](/wiki/Scyld "Scyld") was found as a baby, washed ashore, and adopted by a noble family. The translation is literal and represents the original poetic word order. As such, it is not typical of Old English prose. The modern cognates of original words have been used whenever practical to give a close approximation of the feel of the original poem.

The words in brackets are implied in the Old English by noun case and the bold words in brackets are explanations of words that have slightly different meanings in a modern context. *What* is used by the poet where a word like *lo* or *behold* would be expected. This usage is similar to *what-ho!*, both an expression of surprise and a call to attention.

English poetry is based on stress and alliteration. In alliteration, the first consonant in a word alliterates with the same consonant at the beginning of another word, as with ***G**ār-Dena* and ***ġ**eār-dagum*. Vowels alliterate with any other vowel, as with ***æ**þelingas* and ***e**llen*. In the text below, the letters that alliterate are bolded.

Here is a natural enough Modern English translation, although the phrasing of the Old English passage has often been stylistically preserved, even though it is not usual in Modern English:

> What! We Spear-Danes in ancient days inquired about the glory of the kings of the nation, how the princes performed bravery.
>
> Often, Shield, the son of Sheaf, ripped away the meadbenches
> from many tribes' enemy bands — he terrified men!
>
> After destitution was first experienced (by him), he met with consolation for that; he grew under the clouds of the sky and flourished in adulation, until all of the neighbouring people had to obey him over the whaleroad, and pay tribute to the man. That was a good king!

### Charter of Cnut

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_English&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Charter of Cnut")]

This is a proclamation from [Cnut](/wiki/Cnut "Cnut") to his earl [Thorkell the Tall](/wiki/Thorkell_the_Tall "Thorkell the Tall") and the English people written in AD 1019. Unlike the previous two examples, this text is prose rather than poetry. For ease of reading, the passage has been divided into sentences while the [pilcrows](/wiki/Pilcrow "Pilcrow") represent the original division.

| Original | Representation with constructed cognates |
| --- | --- |
| ¶ *Cnut cyning gret his arcebiscopas and his leod-biscopas and Þurcyl eorl and ealle his eorlas and ealne his þeodscype, tƿelfhynde and tƿyhynde, gehadode and læƿede, on Englalande freondlice.* | ¶ Cnut, king, greets his archbishops and his lede'**(people's)'**-bishops and Thorkell, earl, and all his earls and all his ***people***ship, ***greater*** **(having a 1200 [shilling](/wiki/Shilling "Shilling") [weregild](/wiki/Weregild "Weregild"))** and ***lesser*** **(200 shilling weregild)**, hooded**(ordained to priesthood)** and lewd**(lay)**, in England friendly. |
| *And ic cyðe eoƿ, þæt ic ƿylle beon hold hlaford and unsƿicende to godes gerihtum and to rihtre ƿoroldlage.* | And I kithe**(make known/couth to)** you, that I will be [a] hold**(civilised)** lord and unswiking**(uncheating)** to God's rights**(laws)** and to [the] rights**(laws)** worldly. |

| ¶ *Nu ne ƿandode ic na minum sceattum, þa hƿile þe eoƿ unfrið on handa stod: nu ic mid-godes fultume þæt totƿæmde mid-minum scattum.* | ¶ Now, ne went**(withdrew/changed)** I not my shot**(financial contribution, cf. Norse cognate in scot-free)** the while that you stood**(endured)** unfrith**(turmoil)** on-hand: now I, mid**(with)** God's ***support***, that [unfrith] totwemed**(separated/dispelled)** mid**(with)** my shot**(financial contribution)**. |
| *Þa cydde man me, þæt us mara hearm to fundode, þonne us ƿel licode: and þa for ic me sylf mid-þam mannum þe me mid-foron into Denmearcon, þe eoƿ mæst hearm of com: and þæt hæbbe mid-godes fultume forene forfangen, þæt eoƿ næfre heonon forð þanon nan unfrið to ne cymð, þa hƿile þe ge me rihtlice healdað and min lif byð.* | Tho**(then)** [a] man kithed**(made known/couth to)** me that us more harm ***had*** found**(come upon)** than us well liked**(equalled)**: and tho**(then)** fore**(travelled)** I, meself, mid**(with)** those men that mid**(with)** me fore**(travelled)**, into Denmark that [to] you most harm came of**(from)**: and that[harm] have [I], mid**(with)** God's ***support***, afore**(previously)** forefangen**(forestalled)** that to you never henceforth thence none unfrith**(breach of peace)** ne come the while that ye me rightly hold**(behold as king)** and my life beeth. |

The following is a natural Modern English translation, with the overall structure of the Old English passage preserved. Even though "earl" is used to translate its Old English cognate "eorl", "eorl" in Old English does not correspond exactly to "earl" of the later medieval period:

> King Cnut kindly greets his archbishops and his provincial bishops and Earl Thorkell, and all his earls, and all his people, both those with a weregild of 1,200 shillings and those with a weregild of 200 shillings, both ordained and layman, in England.
>
> And I declare to you, that I will be a kind lord, and faithful to God's laws and to proper secular law.
>
> I recalled the writings and words which the archbishop Lyfing brought to me from the Pope of Rome, that I must promote the worship of God everywhere, and suppress unrighteousness, and promote perfect peace with the power which God would give me.
>
> I never hesitated from my peace payments (e.g. to the Vikings) while you had strife at hand. But with God's help and my payments, that went away.
>
> At that time, I was told that we had been harmed more than we liked; and I departed with the men who accompanied me into Denmark, from where the most harm has come to you; and I have already prevented it with God's help, so that from now on, strife will never come to you from there, while you regard me rightly and my life persists.

## Dictionaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_English&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Dictionaries")]

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_English&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Early history")]

The earliest history of Old English [lexicography](/wiki/Lexicography "Lexicography") lies in the Anglo-Saxon period itself, when English-speaking scholars created English [glosses](/wiki/Glosses "Glosses") on Latin texts. At first, these were often [marginal](/wiki/Marginalia "Marginalia") or [interlinear](/wiki/Interlinear "Interlinear") glosses; however, they soon came to be gathered into word-lists such as the [Épinal-Erfurt](/wiki/%C3%89pinal-Erfurt_glossary "Épinal-Erfurt glossary"), [Leiden](/wiki/Leiden_Glossary "Leiden Glossary") and [Corpus](/wiki/Corpus_Glossary "Corpus Glossary") Glossaries. Over time, these word-lists were consolidated and alphabetised to create extensive Latin–Old English glossaries with some of the character of dictionaries, such as the [Cleopatra Glossaries](/wiki/Cleopatra_Glossaries "Cleopatra Glossaries"), the [Harley Glossary](/wiki/Harley_Glossary "Harley Glossary") and the [Brussels Glossary](/w/index.php?title=Brussels_Glossary&action=edit&redlink=1 "Brussels Glossary (page does not exist)").[[55]](#cite_note-56) In some cases, the material in these glossaries continued to be circulated and updated in [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") glossaries, such as the [Durham Plant-Name Glossary](/wiki/Durham_Plant-Name_Glossary "Durham Plant-Name Glossary") and the [Laud Herbal Glossary](/wiki/Laud_Herbal_Glossary "Laud Herbal Glossary").[[56]](#cite_note-57)

Old English lexicography was revived in the early modern period, drawing heavily on Anglo-Saxons' own glossaries. The major publication at this time was [William Somner](/wiki/William_Somner "William Somner")'s *Dictionarium Saxonico-Latino-Anglicum*.[[57]](#cite_note-58) The next substantial Old English dictionary was [Joseph Bosworth](/wiki/Joseph_Bosworth_(scholar) "Joseph Bosworth (scholar)")'s *Anglo-Saxon Dictionary* of 1838.

### Modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_English&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Modern")]

In modern scholarship, the following dictionaries remain current:

* [Cameron, Angus](/wiki/Angus_Cameron_(academic) "Angus Cameron (academic)"), et al. (ed.) (1983–). *[Dictionary of Old English](/wiki/Dictionary_of_Old_English "Dictionary of Old English")*. Toronto: Published for the Dictionary of Old English Project, Centre for Medieval Studies, University of Toronto by the Pontifical Institute of Medieval Studies. Initially issued on microfiche and subsequently as a CD-ROM, the dictionary is now primarily published online at [https://www.doe.utoronto.ca](https://www.doe.utoronto.ca/). This generally supersedes previous dictionaries where available. As of September 2018, the dictionary covered A-I.
* [Bosworth, Joseph](/wiki/Joseph_Bosworth_(scholar) "Joseph Bosworth (scholar)") and [T. Northcote Toller](/wiki/T._Northcote_Toller "T. Northcote Toller"). (1898). *[An Anglo-Saxon Dictionary](/wiki/An_Anglo-Saxon_Dictionary "An Anglo-Saxon Dictionary")*. Oxford: Clarendon. The main research dictionary for Old English, unless superseded by the *Dictionary of Old English*. Various digitisations are available open-access, including at <http://bosworth.ff.cuni.cz/>. Due to errors and omissions in the 1898 publication, this needs to be read in conjunction with:
  + T. Northcote Toller. (1921). *An Anglo-Saxon Dictionary: Supplement*. Oxford: Clarendon.
  + Alistair Campbell (1972). *An Anglo-Saxon Dictionary: Enlarged addenda and corrigenda*. Oxford: Clarendon.
* [Clark Hall, J. R.](/wiki/Clark_Hall,_J._R. "Clark Hall, J. R.") (1969). *A Concise Anglo-Saxon Dictionary*. 4th rev. edn by Herbet D. Meritt. Cambridge University Press. Occasionally more accurate than Bosworth-Toller, and widely used as a reading dictionary. Various digitisations are available, including [here](https://www.ling.upenn.edu/~kurisuto/germanic/oe_clarkhall_about.html).
* Roberts, Jane and [Christian Kay](/wiki/Christian_Kay "Christian Kay"), with Lynne Grundy, *A Thesaurus of Old English in Two Volumes*, Costerus New Series, 131–32, 2nd rev. impression, 2 vols (Amsterdam: Rodopi, 2000), also available [online](https://oldenglishthesaurus.arts.gla.ac.uk/). A thesaurus based on the definitions in Bosworth-Toller and the structure of *[Roget's Thesaurus](/wiki/Roget%27s_Thesaurus "Roget's Thesaurus")*.

Though focused on later periods, the *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")*, *[Middle English Dictionary](/wiki/Middle_English_Dictionary "Middle English Dictionary")*, *[Dictionary of the Older Scottish Tongue](/wiki/Dictionary_of_the_Older_Scottish_Tongue "Dictionary of the Older Scottish Tongue")*, and *[Historical Thesaurus of English](/wiki/Historical_Thesaurus_of_English "Historical Thesaurus of English")* all also include material relevant to Old English.

## Modern legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_English&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Modern legacy")]

Like other historical languages, Old English has been used by scholars and enthusiasts of later periods to create texts either imitating Old English literature or deliberately transferring it to a different cultural context. Examples include [Alistair Campbell](/wiki/Alistair_Campbell_(academic) "Alistair Campbell (academic)") and [J. R. R. Tolkien](/wiki/J._R._R._Tolkien "J. R. R. Tolkien").[[58]](#cite_note-59) [Ransom Riggs](/wiki/Ransom_Riggs "Ransom Riggs") uses several Old English words, such as syndrigast (singular, peculiar), ymbryne (period, cycle), etc., dubbed as "Old Peculiar" ones. Advocates of [linguistic purism in English](/wiki/Linguistic_purism_in_English "Linguistic purism in English") often look to older forms of English, including Old English, as a means of either reviving old words or coining new ones.

A number of websites devoted to [Modern Paganism](/wiki/Modern_Paganism "Modern Paganism") and [historical reenactment](/wiki/Historical_reenactment "Historical reenactment") offer reference material and forums promoting the active use of Old English. There is also an [Old English version of Wikipedia](https://ang.wikipedia.org/wiki/H%C4%93afodtramet "ang:Hēafodtramet"). However, one investigation found that many Neo-Old English texts published online bear little resemblance to the historical language and have many basic grammatical mistakes.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_English&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-Name_2-0)** By the sixteenth century the term *Anglo-Saxon* came to refer to all things of the early English period, including language, culture, and people. While it remains the normal term for the latter two aspects, the language began to be called Old English towards the end of the 19th century, as a result of the increasingly strong anti-German nationalism in English society of the 1890s and early 1900s. However, many authors still also use the term Anglo-Saxon to refer to the language.[[1]](#cite_note-1)
